questions.question: What is a constructor in Java?
options: A method that returns a new instance of a class
A special method that is automatically called when an object is created with the new keyword
A static method used to initialize class-level variables
A method that must be explicitly called after creating an object
questions.answer: A special method that is automatically called when an object is created with the new keyword

questions.question: How does a constructor differ from a regular method?
options: A constructor must be declared static
A constructor can return any type including void
A constructor has the same name as the class and has no return type, not even void
A constructor must always accept at least one parameter
questions.answer: A constructor has the same name as the class and has no return type, not even void

options: A constructor that initializes name to an empty string and age to 1
A default no-argument constructor that sets fields to their default values
A parameterized constructor accepting name and age
No constructor is provided; the class cannot be instantiated
questions.answer: A default no-argument constructor that sets fields to their default values

questions.question: What happens to the default constructor when you define your own parameterized constructor?
options: The default constructor is still available alongside the parameterized one
The default constructor is automatically updated to call the parameterized constructor
Java no longer provides the default constructor; you must define it explicitly if needed
The default constructor becomes private
questions.answer: Java no longer provides the default constructor; you must define it explicitly if needed

options: label = "", count = 0, price = 0.0, active = false
label = null, count = 0, price = 0.0, active = false
label = null, count = 1, price = 0.0, active = true
All fields are uninitialized and will cause a compilation error
questions.answer: label = null, count = 0, price = 0.0, active = false

questions.question: What does the this keyword refer to inside a constructor?
options: The class itself (the static context)
The superclass of the current object
The current object being constructed
The parameter list of the constructor
questions.answer: The current object being constructed

questions.question: Why is this.name = name used in a constructor when the parameter has the same name as the instance variable?
options: It is optional; Java can automatically distinguish between them
this.name refers to the instance variable and name refers to the parameter, resolving the ambiguity
this.name refers to the parameter and name refers to the instance variable
It creates a new local variable called name
questions.answer: this.name refers to the instance variable and name refers to the parameter, resolving the ambiguity

questions.question: What is constructor overloading?
options: Defining a constructor in a subclass that overrides the parent constructor
Defining multiple constructors in the same class with different parameter lists
Defining a constructor that calls itself recursively
Defining a constructor with the same parameters but a different return type
questions.answer: Defining multiple constructors in the same class with different parameter lists

questions.question: What does this() do when used inside a constructor?
options: It refers to the current object
It calls another constructor in the same class
It calls the parent class constructor
It creates a new instance of the class
questions.answer: It calls another constructor in the same class

questions.question: Where must the this() call appear inside a constructor?
options: It can appear anywhere in the constructor body
It must be the last statement in the constructor
It must be the first statement in the constructor
It must appear inside a conditional statement
questions.answer: It must be the first statement in the constructor

options: Yes, Java provides a default no-arg constructor
Yes, because the field has a default value of 0
No, because there is no no-arg constructor since a parameterized one was defined
No, because the field is private
questions.answer: No, because there is no no-arg constructor since a parameterized one was defined

options: Yes, and it prints Hello then x = 10
Yes, and it prints x = 10 then Hello
No, because this() must be the first statement in the constructor
No, because you cannot have two constructors in one class
questions.answer: No, because this() must be the first statement in the constructor

options: x = 10 and this.x = 99
x = 99 and this.x = 99
x = 99 and this.x = 10
x = 10 and this.x = 10
questions.answer: x = 99 and this.x = 10

questions.question: In the Employee example, what is the purpose of the static field nextId?
options: It stores the name of the next employee to be created
It provides a unique auto-incrementing ID shared across all Employee instances
It limits the number of Employee objects that can be created
It stores the ID of the most recently accessed employee
questions.answer: It provides a unique auto-incrementing ID shared across all Employee instances

questions.question: Given the Employee class with four overloaded constructors, what is the constructor chaining order when new Employee() is called?
options: Full constructor -> three-arg -> two-arg -> no-arg
No-arg -> full constructor -> two-arg -> three-arg
No-arg -> two-arg -> three-arg -> full constructor
Two-arg -> no-arg -> three-arg -> full constructor
questions.answer: No-arg -> two-arg -> three-arg -> full constructor

options: It creates a new Book object with a discounted price
It enables method chaining by returning the current object
It overloads the constructor with a discount parameter
It converts the price to a different currency
questions.answer: It enables method chaining by returning the current object

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid way to call method chaining after applying a discount?
options: Book b = new Book(...); b.applyDiscount(10); b.displayInfo();
Book b = new Book(...); b.applyDiscount(10).displayInfo();
Both of the above are valid and produce the same result
Neither is valid; you cannot chain methods in Java
questions.answer: Both of the above are valid and produce the same result

questions.question: In the Temperature class, what exception should be thrown when an invalid scale character is passed to the constructor?
options: NullPointerException
ArithmeticException
IllegalArgumentException
ClassCastException
questions.answer: IllegalArgumentException

options: No-arg, String: Widget, Int: 5
No-arg, No-arg, No-arg
String: null, String: Widget, Int: 5
Compilation error: ambiguous constructors
questions.answer: No-arg, String: Widget, Int: 5

questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a no-arg constructor that delegates to a two-arg constructor using this()?
options: public Dog() { Dog("Unknown", 0); }
public Dog() { this("Unknown", 0); }
public void Dog() { this("Unknown", 0); }
public Dog() { return new Dog("Unknown", 0); }
questions.answer: public Dog() { this("Unknown", 0); }

questions.question:
What happens if you write a constructor with a return type like void?

public void MyClass() { }
options: It becomes a valid constructor that returns nothing
It is treated as a regular method, not a constructor
It causes a compilation error
It becomes a constructor that can only be called from within the class
questions.answer: It is treated as a regular method, not a constructor

questions.question: In the Temperature class, what is the minimum valid value for Kelvin?
options: There is no minimum for Kelvin
Bob.Smith@company.com
bob.smith@company.com
bobsmith@company.com
BOB.SMITH@company.com
questions.answer: bob.smith@company.com

questions.question: Which statement about constructors and the new keyword is correct?
options: You can call a constructor directly without using new
The new keyword allocates memory for the object and then invokes the constructor
The new keyword is optional when a no-arg constructor exists
The new keyword calls the constructor first and then allocates memory
questions.answer: The new keyword allocates memory for the object and then invokes the constructor
